title: Create VectorizeMemory.bx — BoxLang class wrapping mxaiVectorizeUpsert/Query to mirror BXAI IVectorMemory API
description:
Create packages/mx-ai/VectorizeMemory.bx — a BoxLang class that wraps mxaiVectorizeUpsert() and mxaiVectorizeQuery() to match BXAI's IVectorMemory interface:

Methods:
- configure(config) — set bindingName, collection, dimensions, embeddingModel, metric
- getRelevant(query, limit=5, filter={}, minScore=0.0) — embed via aiEmbed() -> mxaiVectorizeQuery() -> filter by minScore -> look up text from D1 -> return [{id, text, score, metadata, embedding}]
- findSimilar(embedding, limit=5, filter={}) — mxaiVectorizeQuery() raw -> return matches
- addWithId(id, text, metadata={}) — embed via aiEmbed() -> mxaiVectorizeUpsert() -> store text in D1
- add(message) — extract text + metadata from message struct -> addWithId()
- seed(documents) — batch add multiple documents
- getById(id) — lookup from D1
- remove(id) — Vectorize deletion + D1 cleanup
- count() — count from Vectorize stats or D1
- getConfig(), clear(), getAll() — lifecycle methods

Must preserve the EXACT return format: [{ id, text, score, metadata, embedding }]. Score conversion: Vectorize cosine distance is 0-2 range where 0=identical. Convert to BXAI's 0-1 scale: score = 1 - (distance / 2).
close_reason: Closed

2) On message: embed query via mxaiEmbed() -> mxaiVectorizeQuery() with topK=5 -> build context from returned chunk text -> inject into OpenRouter system prompt.

3) Remove the keyword-based extractKeywords/matchScore/findIn functions.

4) Update skychat wrangler.toml to add [[vectorize]] binding.

5) Update skychat mcf-worker.js to support 'vectorize_upsert'/'vectorize_query' actions.

6) Create Vectorize index: skybox-skychat-index (--dimensions=768 --metric=cosine).

7) Update schema.sql: vector_store_chunks now stores Vectorize metadata refs instead of just keywords.

8) Client-side: RAG debug panel shows Vectorize scores instead of keyword match counts.

2) case 'vectorize_query': handleVectorizeQuery(msg) - calls env.VECTORIZE.query(queryVector, {topK, returnMetadata, returnValues}). Returns matches array with id, score, metadata, values.

Also add [[vectorize]] binding to shell wrangler.toml template. After updating shell, copy to all example dirs that need it (skychat, boxdox).

title: Implement mxaiVectorizeUpsert and mxaiVectorizeQuery Rust BIFs
description:
Create new Rust BIFs in bifs.rs:

1) mxaiVectorizeUpsert(bindingName, vectors) — async future BindingCall with action 'vectorize_upsert'. Vectors arg is an array of {id, values: [float...], metadata: {...}}. Sends to JS shell which calls env.VECTORIZE.upsert().

2) mxaiVectorizeQuery(bindingName, queryVector, topK, options) — async future BindingCall with action 'vectorize_query'. queryVector is a float array. topK limits results. Options can include returnMetadata, returnValues, filter. JS shell calls env.VECTORIZE.query(). Returns matches with score, id, metadata.

Both follow the same async future pattern as d1Query/mxaiEmbed: next_async_id() -> CalloutMessage::BindingCall -> PENDING_OPS -> vm.set_async_waiting().

title: Phase 2: BoxLang AI Chat Listener
description:
Write the BoxLang listener class at packages/mx-ai/examples/skychat/SkychatListener.bx. The listener calls openRouterChat() Rust BIF for AI chat, which delegates to JS via the async callout bridge.

**Architecture Update**: The openRouterChat() BIF is now a Rust-level BIF registered in crates/matchbox-cf-worker/src/bifs.rs. The BoxLang listener only needs to build message JSON and call the BIF — streaming is handled by JS.

**Class structure**:
class SkychatListener {
    function onConnect(required channel) { }
    function onMessage(required message, required channel) { }
    function onClose(required channel) { }
    function onHttpGet(required struct request) { }
}

**State** (lazy-init pattern):
- users: struct keyed by connectionId, each with {id, nickname, room}
- rooms: struct keyed by room name
- userCount: counter for default nicknames

**Commands** (text-based):
- /ai <message> -> call openRouterChat("OPENROUTER", messagesJson, connectionId)
- /nick <name> -> change nickname
- /help -> show commands
- Plain text -> broadcast to room

**AI Chat flow**:
1. Parse /ai <message> from text
2. Build messages array as JSON: serializeJSON([{role:"system",content:"..."},{role:"user",content:"..."}])
3. Call: openRouterChat("OPENROUTER", messagesJson, channel.getConnectionId())
4. Rust BIF sends BindingCall to JS
5. JS streams SSE chunks directly to client via sendToWS()
6. JS calls vm_complete_async() to resume VM with final result

**Note**: This is a dependency of the mx-ai package BIF stubs at packages/mx-ai/bifs/.
close_reason: Closed

title: Phase 1: Rust BIF + JS Bridge for OpenRouter HTTP fetch
description:
Add the async callout bridge for OpenRouter HTTP requests:

**Rust side (bifs.rs)**:
- Add `openRouterChat` BIF: takes (bindingName, messagesJson, connectionId)
- Follow the same async pattern as `d1_query_bif` / `d1_execute_bif`
- Sends a BindingCall with action "openrouter", args: {messages, connection_id}
- Creates a future, yields the VM via set_async_waiting(async_id)
- Register the BIF in register_bifs()

**JS side (mcf-worker.js)**:
- Add `__skybox_binding_call` global handler function
- Route by action type: "d1" -> existing D1, "openrouter" -> new handler
- OpenRouter handler:
  - Read OPENROUTER_API_KEY from env
  - Make streaming fetch to https://openrouter.ai/api/v1/chat/completions
  - Model: "openrouter/free"
  - Read SSE stream line by line via ReadableStream
  - For each chunk with delta.content: call instance.sendToWS(connectionId, chunk)
  - On stream end: call vm_complete_async with full response text
  - On error: send error message to client, resume VM

**WASM exports**: No new exports needed — vm_complete_async already exists.
close_reason: openRouterChat BIF designed, async bridge mapped, mcf-worker.js extension planned in Phase 1

title: [epic] Full D1 Binding Bridge + Async VM + Quick ORM + Todo Demo
description:
## Goal
Build a complete D1 database bridge for BoxLang on WASM/Cloudflare Workers, including:
- Async-aware VM fiber system that can pause/resume for async callouts
- Generic Cloudflare binding callout bridge (D1 now, R2/KV/Queues later)
- D1 BIFs: d1Query(), d1Execute()
- Quick-style ActiveRecord ORM for WASM (QuickEntity.bx)
- Todo demo app with REST API + web UI + .bxm templates + assets
- Deploy to Cloudflare Workers

## Key Design Decisions
- VM pause-and-resume: exports return paused state, JS calls vm_resume with results
- Generic BindingCall multiplexes on binding name + action
- Fiber yield + native completions for async support
- No WebSockets for todo app — pure HTTP REST API

## Phases
1. Generic Binding Callout Bridge (Rust + JS)
2. D1 BIFs + pause-and-resume VM protocol
3. Quick-style ActiveRecord ORM (BoxLang)
4. Todo demo app (Application.bx, .bxm, assets, REST API)
5. Build, test, deploy with D1
close_reason: Todo demo deployed and working at https://matchbox-todo.c0d3t3k.workers.dev. Full CRUD: POST/GET/PATCH/DELETE, root page with JS UI, DO persistence. D1 async bridge deferred (BoxLang future .get() crashes on WASM).

title: Port aiDocuments.bx and Document.bx from BXAI refs to MXAI as pure BoxLang
description:
Port the document loading system from refs/bx-ai/ to packages/mx-ai/. Key files:

1) models/loaders/Document.bx — document value object with content, metadata, embedding. Methods: setContent(), getContentLength(), getTokenCount(), chunk(), toStruct(), getHash(), equals(). No JVM deps — direct port.

2) models/loaders/IDocumentLoader.bx — interface

3) models/loaders/BaseDocumentLoader.bx — abstract base (may have JVM deps, strip as needed)

4) models/loaders/TextLoader.bx, MarkdownLoader.bx, JSONLoader.bx — simple format-specific loaders

5) bifs/aiDocuments.bx — factory BIF: aiDocuments(source, config). Auto-detects loader type from extension/content.

For MXAI, file-based loaders (TextLoader, MarkdownLoader) won't work (no FS on Workers). But Document.bx (value object), JSONLoader (parsing), and aiDocuments factory can work as stubs or in-memory loaders. The Document.chunk() method is the most important piece.

Focus on: Document.bx, aiDocuments.bx factory, toMemory() pipeline integration with VectorizeMemory.

value: The D1 async bridge works via a pause/resume protocol: BIFs (d1Query/d1Execute) create a BoxLang Future, send a BindingCall callout to JS with async_id, and set async_waiting on the fiber. The VM exports return {__paused__: true, ops: [...]} to the JS shell. The JS shell's handleBindingCall starts the D1 promise and stores it in pendingAsyncOps. handleHttpRequest loops: await pending ops, call vm_complete_async, repeat until no __paused__. D1 BIFs in bifs.rs, callout bridge in do_adapter.rs, pause/resume in JS mcf-worker.js. structKeyExists/structKeyList/structCount/chr/deserializeJSON NOT available on WASM. Use serializeJSON-based struct introspection instead. QuickEntity.bx in sources/ dir.

One DO per app: MatchBoxWebSocketDO uses Hibernation API with one BoxLang VM shared across all WebSocket connections. Broadcast via ctx.getWebSockets() iteration. State persists via DO storage (listener_state + connections keys).

## bx-ai API Surface Mapping (for mx-ai port)

This is the complete API surface of the `bx-ai` JVM BoxLang module (refs/bx-ai/), mapped for porting to `mx-ai` (MatchBox/WASM SkyBox package at packages/mx-ai/).

### Core Chat BIFs (IMPLEMENT for streaming)

|-----|-----------|--------|

SkyChat (examples/skychat) is a streaming AI chat app using OpenRouter free model. Architecture: Rust BIF openRouterChat sends BindingCall to JS __skybox_binding_call handler, which streams SSE chunks directly to WebSocket client via sendToWS(), then calls vm_complete_async() to resume BoxLang VM. Uses same async future pattern as D1 bridge in bifs.rs.

Cloudflare's official RAG stack is Workers AI + Vectorize + D1. SkyBox MXAI now has Vectorize BIFs (mxaiVectorizeUpsert, mxaiVectorizeQuery) that call env.VECTORIZE.upsert()/query() via the BindingCall bridge. This replaces the earlier plan of storing embeddings as D1 BLOBs and computing cosine similarity in Rust. Key benefits: globally distributed vector search, no manual similarity computation, built-in metadata filtering, high-precision scoring. Create Vectorize indexes with: npx wrangler vectorize create <name> --dimensions=768 --metric=cosine. There's also AI Search — a fully managed RAG layer — but Vectorize is the right fit for the demo since we control the query flow and can chain with D1 lookups. Both skychat and boxdox demos use this pattern.

SkyChat uses fire-and-forget openRouterChat Rust BIF (not async futures). JS __skybox_binding_call handler does streaming SSE via sendToWS() directly to client. No vm_complete_async needed. Use jsonserialize() not serializeJSON() on WASM (case-sensitive BIF lookup). Try/catch unavailable on WASM BoxLang compiler.

WASM BIF limitations: deserializeJSON, reReplace, replace, asc, chr, int, val, dateFormat, pi, sin, abs are NOT available on wasm32-unknown-unknown. Use manual alternatives: character comparison (c >= 'a'), manual string iteration, structural JSON validation.

Workerd testing: Each example has test_{name}.capnp (workerd config) and test_{name}.js (ES module). Tests create a DO by calling initSync → vm_init → vm_on_connect → vm_on_message. The __skybox_send/broadcast/close globals capture responses. Assertions check JSON response types and values.

## MXAI RAG & ORM Implementation Plan

### Feasibility Assessment: HIGHLY FEASIBLE

All building blocks exist. Adding Turso support is just adding a new transport layer — same SQL, same abstractions.

### Engineering Philosophy

See AGENTS.md "Engineering Philosophy: Leverage Before Build" section. Default to crates/libraries where sensible.

### Architecture: Composable Adapter Layers

The key insight: D1 and Turso are BOTH SQLite. Same SQL syntax, same types. Only the transport differs.

```
┌─────────────────────────────────────────────────┐
│  BoxLang Application Layer                      │
│  QuickORM (findAll, insert, update, delete)     │
│  VectorMemory (getRelevant, addWithId, seed)    │
├─────────────────────────────────────────────────┤
│  DatabaseAdapter Interface                     │
│  query(sql, params) -> rows                    │
│  execute(sql, params) -> affected              │
│  batch(statements) -> results                  │
├───────────────────┬─────────────────────────────┤
│  D1Adapter         │  TursoAdapter              │
│  (d1Query BIF)     │  (tursoQuery BIF)          │
├───────────────────┴─────────────────────────────┤
│  Rust BIF Layer                                 │
│  d1Query/d1Execute  │  tursoQuery/tursoExecute  │
│  (existing)          │  (new)                    │
├───────────────────┴─────────────────────────────┤
│  JS Binding Call Dispatch                       │
│  "query"/"execute"  │  "turso_query"/"execute"  │
│  -> env.DB.prepare  │  -> fetch(Turso HTTP API) │
└─────────────────────────────────────────────────┘
```

### DatabaseAdapter Interface (BoxLang)

```
interface {
  query(sql, params=[]) -> array of struct rows
  execute(sql, params=[]) -> numeric affected
  batch(statements) -> array of results
}
```

Implementations:
- **D1Adapter** — wraps existing d1Query/d1Execute Rust BIFs (async future pattern, already proven)
- **TursoAdapter** — wraps new tursoQuery/tursoExecute BIFs (async future -> JS fetch to Turso HTTP API)

### QuickORM Uses DatabaseAdapter

```
class QuickEntity {
  init(tableName, adapter)
  findAll() / findById(id)
  insert(data) / update(id, data)
  deleteById(id) / deleteAll()
  createTable(schema)
}
```

The adapter is injected via constructor. QuickORM never touches D1 or Turso directly.

### Vector Memory Uses DatabaseAdapter

```
class D1VectorMemory extends BaseVectorMemory {
  init(adapter, collection, embeddingProvider)
  // storeDocument -> adapter.execute(INSERT INTO vectors ...)
  // searchByVector -> adapter.query(SELECT * FROM vectors) + cosine similarity
  // getAllDocuments -> adapter.query(SELECT * FROM vectors WHERE collection = ?)
}
```

Same SQL works for both backends since they are both SQLite. The DatabaseAdapter handles transport.

For Turso, the vector SQL is identical to D1:
- CREATE TABLE vectors (id TEXT PK, collection TEXT, text TEXT, embedding TEXT, metadata TEXT, user_id TEXT, conversation_id TEXT, created_at TEXT)
- SELECT * FROM vectors WHERE collection = ?
- INSERT INTO vectors (id, collection, text, embedding, metadata, ...) VALUES (?, ?, ?, ?, ?, ...)

### Crates & Rust Code Decisions

| Component | Approach | Why |
|-----------|----------|-----|
| Cosine similarity | 3-line Rust BIF (manual) or innr crate | Both WASM-compatible. innr gives SIMD if desired. Manual is simpler for MVP. |
| D1 bridge | No new crate needed | Existing serde_json + wasm-bindgen |
| Turso HTTP bridge | No new crate needed | HTTP fetch from JS, not Rust |
| Embedding cache key | sha2 (already in matchbox_vm deps) | No new dep needed |

### Key Technical Decisions

1. **DatabaseAdapter is the seam**: Both QuickORM and VectorMemory take a DatabaseAdapter. To swap D1 for Turso, change one line: `new D1VectorMemory(new D1Adapter())` -> `new D1VectorMemory(new TursoAdapter())`.
2. **Turso transport**: JS shell does fetch() to Turso HTTP API with Bearer token auth. Async future pattern (like D1), NOT fire-and-forget (like OpenRouter).
3. **Same SQL**: D1 and Turso are both SQLite. No SQL differences needed.
4. **Turso config**: TURSO_URL + TURSO_AUTH_TOKEN in DO env (set in wrangler.toml as secrets).
5. **wrangler.toml for Turso**: Env vars, not bindings (Turso is not a CF-native binding).

## Plan: SkyBox AI Chat on Cloudflare Workers using OpenRouter

### Overview
Build a streaming AI chat application using OpenRouter's free model router, deployed to Cloudflare Workers via SkyBox. The app uses a WebSocket-based BoxLang listener on MatchBox/WASM, with a Rust-to-JS async callout bridge for HTTP requests to OpenRouter.

### The Critical Constraint
The `bx-ai` package (`aiChat()`, `aiChatStream()`, `aiModel()`) is a **JVM BoxLang module** and is **NOT available** on MatchBox/WASM. MatchBox has no HTTP client capability and a limited BIF set. Therefore, the BX-AI package cannot be used directly. Instead, we build an equivalent callout bridge: a Rust BIF sends a binding call to JS, JS makes the OpenRouter HTTP fetch, and pushes streaming chunks directly to the client WebSocket.

### Architecture

```
Client WebSocket  ←→  [mcf-worker.js DO]  ←→  Rust VM (WASM)  ←→  BoxLang Listener
                            ↕  (HTTP fetch via callout bridge)
                      OpenRouter API (openrouter.ai)
```

Data flow for an AI chat request:
1. Client sends chat message via WebSocket
2. DO's `webSocketMessage()` calls `vm_on_message()`
3. BoxLang `onMessage()` recognizes AI chat intent
4. BoxLang calls `openRouterChat(binding, messages, connectionId)` (new Rust BIF)
5. Rust sends `BindingCall` message to JS via `__skybox_binding_call`
6. JS makes streaming HTTP POST to OpenRouter
7. For each SSE chunk, JS calls `sendToWS(connectionId, chunk)` to stream to client
8. JS calls `vm_complete_async(resultsJson)` to resume VM with the final response
9. BoxLang receives final result, optionally broadcasts or stores it

### Phase 0: Foundation
- Fork/extend the chatroom example as a starting template
- Set up the project structure: `examples/skychat/`
- Add `OPENROUTER_API_KEY` environment variable handling
- Create `package.json`, `wrangler.toml`, `state.json`
- `wrangler.toml` name: `skybox-skychat`

### Phase 1: Rust BIF + JS Bridge (HTTP Fetch)
**Rust side** (`bifs.rs`):
- Add `openRouterChat` BIF: takes (binding_name, messages_json, connection_id)
- Creates an async future, sends `BindingCall` to JS, yields VM
- `BindingCall`: `{action: "openrouter", binding_name, args: {messages, connection_id}}`

**JS side** (`mcf-worker.js`):
- Add `__skybox_binding_call` handler function
- Route binding calls by `action`:
  - `d1`: existing D1 handling
  - `openrouter`: new OpenRouter handler
- OpenRouter handler:
  - Calls `fetch('https://openrouter.ai/api/v1/chat/completions')` with `stream: true`
  - Reads SSE response via `TextDecoderStream` + line reader
  - For each chunk with `delta.content`: calls `instance.sendToWS(connectionId, chunk)`
  - On stream end: calls `vm_complete_async`
  - On error: sends error message to client

**WASM exports** (`wasm_exports.rs`):
- `vm_complete_async` already exists (used for D1) — reuse for OpenRouter

### Phase 2: BoxLang AI Chat Listener
**`SkychatListener.bx`**:
- Class with `onConnect`, `onMessage`, `onClose`, `onHttpGet`
- State management: user registry, chat rooms, message history
- Command routing: `/ai <message>` for AI chat, `/nick <name>`, `/help`
- On `/ai` message:
  - Build system prompt: `You are a helpful assistant. Keep responses concise.`
  - Build messages array: `[{role:"system", content}, {role:"user", content}]`
  - Call `openRouterChat("OPENROUTER", serializeJSON(messages), connectionId)`
  - Wait for async completion (VM yields, JS streams, VM resumes)
  - Optionally log/store the final response

**`state.json`**: `{"room":"lobby","userCount":0,"users":{},"messages":[]}`

### Phase 3: Frontend Web UI
- HTML page served via `onHttpGet` (rendered inline like chatroom)
- Chat UI with:
  - Message display area (user messages + AI responses streaming in)
  - Text input + send button
  - Sidebar for rooms/users
  - `/ai` prefix or button toggle for AI mode
- JavaScript WebSocket client:
  - Connect, reconnection with backoff
  - Send messages, receive + render streaming responses
  - SSE-style chunk rendering: append partial AI responses as they arrive
  - Command input: `/nick`, `/help`, `/ai <prompt>`
- CSS styling (dark theme, bubbles, responsive)

### Phase 4: Streaming Support
**Challenge**: The BoxLang VM is synchronous. The JS streaming approach works because:
1. JS directly pushes chunks to the WebSocket client via `sendToWS()`
2. The BoxLang VM is paused waiting for the async result
3. When streaming completes, JS resumes the VM with the final result

**Implementation details**:
- SSE chunk format: `data: {"choices":[{"delta":{"content":"text"}}]}`
- JS reads line by line, parses `data:` lines
- Extracts `choices[0].delta.content`, sends to client as `{"type":"ai_chunk","content":"..."}`
- On `[DONE]` or `finish_reason: "stop"`: sends `{"type":"ai_done","content":"..."}`
- Client JS appends chunks to the AI message bubble in real-time

### Phase 5: Deployment
- Build: `bash build-multi.sh` or equivalent
- `wrangler.toml` configuration:
  - `name = "skybox-skychat"`
  - `main = "mcf-worker.js"`
  - `compatibility_date = "2025-01-01"`

  - `[[migrations]]` with `MatchBoxWebSocketDO`
  - `[assets]` with `directory = "dist/assets"`
- Environment variable: `OPENROUTER_API_KEY`
- Deploy: `npx wrangler deploy`

### BIFs Used in BoxLang (WASM-available subset)
Already available: `trim`, `len`, `lcase`, `ucase`, `listToArray`, `arrayAppend`, `arrayLen`, `isNull`, `isStruct`, `isNumeric`, `serializeJSON`, `structNew`, `arrayNew`, `toString`, `now`, string concat (`&`), comparison ops
New: `openRouterChat(binding, messagesJson, connectionId)` — custom Rust BIF

### OpenRouter API Details (for reference)
- Endpoint: `POST https://openrouter.ai/api/v1/chat/completions`
- Headers: `Authorization: Bearer ${OPENROUTER_API_KEY}`, `Content-Type: application/json`
- Free model: `model: "openrouter/free"` (auto-selects best free model)
- Streaming: `stream: true` → SSE response
- Body: `{model: "openrouter/free", messages: [...], stream: true}`
- SSE chunks: `data: {"choices":[{"delta":{"content":"..."}}]}`
- Terminator: `data: [DONE]`

### Why Not BX-AI Directly?
The `bx-ai` package requires:
- JVM BoxLang runtime (not MatchBox)
- HTTP client capability (not available on WASM)
- Module loading infrastructure
Therefore, we build a lightweight equivalent using the Rust→JS async callout bridge.

Variable persistence pattern: On WASM, class-level variable initialization (variables.xxx = {...}) does not persist across restarts. Use lazy-init with variables.xxx = (variables.xxx ?: default) inside onConnect.
